ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Rakhine thoke noodle salad. FOODON_00002473 (noodle dish) is used as the closest parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (noodle-based dishes) is the closest parent. No specific code exists for Rakhine-style noodle salad.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Rakhine chicken salad noodles is approximately 350 g, comprising a generous portion of rice noodles tossed with shredded chicken, aromatics, and dressing.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated from component analysis: rice noodles (USDA FoodData Central #20066), cooked chicken breast (USDA FoodData Central #05064), fish sauce, shallots, dried shrimp, sesame oil, lime juice, and chilli — weighted by typical Rakhine thoke recipe proportions
nutrient_source.organization: USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy / Foundation Foods)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for Rakhine chicken thoke exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component ingredients weighted by a representative recipe: approximately 45% rice noodles (cooked), 25% shredded chicken breast, 10% shallots/garlic/aromatics, 10% dressing (fish sauce, lime, sesame oil), 10% dried shrimp and chilli. Energy calculated using Atwater factors.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of rice noodles and analogous mixed noodle salad dishes — University of Sydney GI Database; rice noodles GI ~53 (Atkinson FS et al., 2008, Diabetes Care)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Rakhine chicken thoke. GI of 53 is estimated from the GI of cooked rice noodles (GI ~53, low category) as the dominant carbohydrate source, per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008, Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283). The protein and fat content from chicken and sesame oil will further attenuate the postprandial glycaemic response. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (53 × 64.75) / 100 ≈ 34.3, where carbohydrate per 350 g serving = 18.5 × 3.5 = 64.75 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish sauce and dried shrimp
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced fish sauce, as a 350 g serving provides approximately 1,470 mg sodium — nearly 64% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2,300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycaemic load from rice noodles, partially offset by protein and fat
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware that a standard serving carries a glycaemic load of approximately 34, which is moderate-to-high; pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables and reducing noodle portion can help moderate postprandial blood glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat from chicken and sesame oil
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, the high sodium content warrants caution; however, the use of lean chicken breast and rice noodles keeps saturated fat relatively low at approximately 2.5 g per serving, which is acceptable within a heart-healthy diet if sodium is managed.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Rakhine Chicken Salad Noodles (ရူးရှဲကြက်သားရခိုင်သုတ်, romanized: Ruu Shae Kyar Thar Rakhine Thoke) is a traditional cold-tossed noodle salad originating from Rakhine State (formerly Arakan) in western Myanmar. The dish features thin rice noodles — typically round rice vermicelli or flat rice noodles — tossed while warm with shredded or sliced poached chicken breast, dried shrimp, crispy fried shallots, raw garlic, fresh chilli, turmeric, fish sauce, lime juice, and a drizzle of sesame oil. The result is a vibrant, tangy, umami-rich salad with a firm yet slippery noodle texture, fragrant aromatics, and a balance of sour, salty, and spicy flavours characteristic of Rakhine cuisine. Unlike many Burmese noodle dishes that are served in broth, Rakhine thoke is a dry salad preparation, making it lighter in appearance but intensely flavoured. The dish is commonly eaten as a main meal at lunch or dinner and is widely available in Myanmar restaurants across Southeast Asia, including Singapore. Nutritionally, a standard 350 g restaurant serving provides approximately 483 kcal, 32 g protein, 11 g fat, and 65 g carbohydrate. The sodium content is notably high (approximately 1,470 mg per serving) due to fish sauce and dried shrimp, which is a key concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycaemic index of the rice noodle base is approximately 53 (low category), though the glycaemic load per serving is moderate-to-high at approximately 34. The dish is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free, and is suitable for halal diets when prepared with halal-certified chicken and fish sauce.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for Rakhine chicken thoke exists in any approved primary regional database (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component ingredients using representative recipe proportions. GI is estimated by analogy from rice noodles per the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source data for the component ingredients used in this estimation. 2. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g per 100 g; this is confirmed zero based on the absence of hydrogenated fats in the traditional recipe formulation. 3. The food_group 'Grains and staples' is preserved from the input record as the dominant macronutrient is carbohydrate from rice noodles. 4. The cuisine_tags 'Lor Mee' from the input field survey record appear to be a platform tagging error; this dish is not Lor Mee (a Chinese-Hokkien braised noodle dish) but a Rakhine dry noodle salad. The tag has not been carried forward. 5. GI of 53 is an analogous estimate based on rice noodles as the primary carbohydrate source; no direct GI measurement for Rakhine thoke was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of the accessed date. 6. Sodium content is high due to fish sauce and dried shrimp; individuals on sodium-restricted diets should exercise caution. 7. Halal status assumes use of halal-certified fish sauce and chicken; restaurant-specific preparation may vary.
